findings.description: The HTML includes fabricated styling/theme content and branding not present in the source PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML contains CSS comments/text such as "Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS", font imports, and variables like "--nc-navy", "--nc-blue", and "--nc-link". The PDF pages only show the title "APPLICATION TIME STANDARDS 2006" and the date table, with no Digital Commons branding or theme text.

findings.description: The HTML merges the multi-page table into one continuous table and drops many rows from the PDF, causing table data loss.
findings.evidence: The PDF spans 5 pages of date rows beginning on page 1 with "01/01/06 ... 02/10/06" and continuing through page 5 to "12/26/06". In the HTML, after row "02/09/06 ... 03/21/06 ... 09/21/06", it jumps directly to "03/22/06 ... 05/01/06", omitting the PDF rows for 02/10/06 through 03/21/06. Similar gaps appear later: after "04/30/06 ... 06/09/06 ... 12/09/06" it jumps to "06/10/06 ... 07/20/06", omitting 05/01/06 through 06/09/06; after "07/19/06 ... 08/28/06" it jumps to "08/29/06 ... 10/08/06", omitting 07/20/06 through 08/28/06; after "10/07/06 ... 11/16/06" it jumps to "11/17/06 ... 12/27/06", omitting 10/08/06 through 11/16/06.

findings.description: Text/data accuracy is compromised because large ranges of dates from the source table are missing in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF page sequence shows continuous daily entries across the year, but the HTML skips entire date ranges, e.g., PDF page 2 starts with rows around "03/22/06" while page 1 should have ended at "03/21/06"; PDF page 3 starts with "06/10/06" while HTML lacks the intermediate rows from "05/01/06" to "06/09/06". These omissions change the meaning and completeness of the reference table.

findings.description: Some content appears duplicated or structurally repeated unnecessarily in the HTML wrapper.
findings.evidence: The HTML contains nested main elements: one outer "<main>" wrapping a header, then another inner "<main>" around the table. While the PDF has a simple single-page layout repeated across pages, the HTML introduces redundant structural wrappers not reflected in the source.
